ground_truth.title: Rust GCC backend: Why and how
ground_truth.author: Guillaume Gomez
ground_truth.main_content:
Whenever you compile using Rust, the compiler goes through different passes and in the end, generated binary code for the target processor. By default, it uses LLVM as backend to generate the binary code, but more backends exist like cranelift and GCC. This post is about how it's possible for one compiler to use different backend to generate binaries, in particular GCC.

We consider the front-end to be the part handling code parsing, linting, type-checking and borrow-checking. When all this is done, it means the code is valid and needs to be translated to the target processor instructions set. To do so, we call LLVM/GCC which will translate the Rust compiler AST into assembly code.

LLVM being much more recent than GCC (2003 vs 1987), a lot of older processors are not supported and will never be. So if you want to write a Rust program on an old platform like Dreamcast, you have no choice to either write your own backend or use the GCC backend.

The GCC backend (the crate name is rustc_codegen_gcc) is just "yet another backend codegen" of the Rust compiler, like LLVM or Cranelift, only meant to generate the binary from the Rust compiler input. It's a bridge between Rust compiler's AST and the codegen API.

Rustc has a crate named rustc_codegen_ssa which provides an abstract interface that a backend needs to implement through traits like CodegenBackend, ExtraBackendMethods, and WriteBackendMethods.

So now you know what a Rust backend is, and why GCC backend is also an interesting thing to have while also learning about some optimizations we do behind developers back.
with: Whenever you compile using Rust, the compiler goes through different passes and in the end, generated binary code for the target processor.
We consider the front-end to be the part handling code parsing, linting, type-checking and borrow-checking.
LLVM being much more recent than GCC (2003 vs 1987), a lot of older processors are not supported and will never be.
The GCC backend is different than gccrs which is a front-end for GCC written in C++, which doesn't reuse the front-end of rustc.
This is the function that will be called by rustc to run your backend.
